stimulus:
The following text is adapted from Tommy Orange’s 2018 novel There There. Edwin Black is collecting interview pledges for an oral-history project from fellow members of his urban Native community in Oakland.

Edwin Black tended the clipboard like a garden. He kept it with him at all times. Around the neighborhood, his eyes brightened when he spotted a community member who hadn’t yet signed up. Wherever they were—at the laundromat, the corner store, at the powwow committee meeting, on the sidewalk, or outside the rec center—Edwin cornered them. If they were holding a sleeping baby, he’d have them sign up. If they were laughing, if they were arguing. If they were walking a child home from after-school, they signed up.

©2018 by Tommy Orange
stem: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?

answerOptions.content: To suggest that some community members refuse to sign up because they dislike Edwin Black
To portray Edwin Black’s strong commitment to collecting interview pledges
To show that attitudes toward the project within the community change over time
To demonstrate that most community members are enthusiastic about signing up

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer because it most accurately states the text’s main purpose. The text indicates that Edwin always had his clipboard with him, sought out community members who hadn’t signed up, and lists several comical circumstances in which he would press for a pledge. Thus, the main purpose is to portray Edwin’s dedicated focus on collecting interview pledges.

Choice A is incorrect because the text addresses Edwin’s pursuit, not how community members feel about him. Choice C is incorrect because the text does not trace any change in attitudes over time. Choice D is incorrect because the text does not say that members are enthusiastic; the word “cornered” suggests their enthusiasm was neither needed nor considered.
